skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A sailing vessel defined by a combination of such factors as hull design, locality and rig.
A craft with two masts.It is usually cutter rigged on the foremast and has a small mizzen mast.
A vessel used to maintain supply or assist larger vessels. Use more specific term.
A vessel used to carry supplies and equipment needed by recreational or deep sea divers.
A tug equipped with water tanks and pumps for fighting fires at sea or on the shore line.
A small and powerful vessel used to assist vessels that are sinking or disabled at sea.
A relatively small and heavily built vessel equipped with large engines. Used for towing ships at sea or manouevring them in tight spaces.
A rescue tug equipped to care for survivors from torpedoed vessels and also available for towing to port ships damaged by enemy action.
Craft used to maintain, supply or assist larger vessels whilst in harbour.
17th century Dutch vessel built for naval or East India service, with a relatively flat bottom and narrow hull; sometimes rendered into English as `fly-boat'.
A craft used for the transportation of cargoes. Use specific type where known.
A craft designed for use on canals and inland waterways. Usually 70ft long x 7ft wide though can be longer and broader.
A vessel, usually a merchant vessel, altered to resemble a warship or another vessel.
A vessel used to carry essential supplies, such as food and armaments, to naval ships in order to keep them replenished whilst on patrol. Use more specific term.
A civil vessel disguised as a warship.
A small merchant ship or warship with concealed weapons, intended as a trap for enemy submarines.
A ship that is built and primarily intended for war.
A small fast warship developed in the late nineteenth century. Armed principally with quick firing guns to destroy enemy torpedo boats.
A very fast small warship, armed with small calibre guns and torpedoes. Used for attacking enemy vessels, escort duties and anti submarine duties.
A ship or brig peculiar to the Mediterranean.
A barge with a lugsail rig commonly found on the River Thames in the late 19th century. Also a general term applied to the family of Deal luggers.
From 1822, any vessel in the service of HM Coastguard, principally deployed on Customs and Revenue duties, i.e. anti-smuggling operations. Superseded the Preventive Service Vessel.
The class term, use more specific terms.
A small vessel used as transport for customs officers.
Prior to 1822 a vessel used by the customs department involved with the prevention of smuggling.

Any vessel in the service of one of the historical or modern agencies concerned with the prevention of inbound or outbound smuggling: namely the Preventive Service, the Coastguard, or the Customs and Excise. Use more specific term.
A relatively small and heavily built vessel equipped with large engines. Used for towing ships at sea or manouevring them in tight spaces.
A rescue tug equipped to care for survivors from torpedoed vessels and also available for towing to port ships damaged by enemy action.
A vessel used for the laying and removal of buoys and other navigational markers.
Small landing craft often armoured, designed to carry infantrymen and their equipment ashore from larger vessels offshore.
Inshore craft used for carrying troops and military equipment used either for beach landings or as a ferry. Use specific type where known.
A large ship's boat powered by oars and or an engine.
A type of small boat that was often carried, or towed by a larger vessel and used as a ship's boat. Dinghies can also be used recreationally as independent sailing vessels.
All vessels, use more specific type where known.

Satellite craft belonging to a larger vessel, usually a rowing vessel; used to pick up or set down passengers or crew when lying offshore, or stowed on board as the "long boat" or "jolly boat" for lifesaving purposes.
Warships constructed of iron plate or with wooden hulls covered by iron plates. Used as a generic name for the first examples of armour plated warships.
A warship constructed of iron plate with its main armament mounted in a revolving turret on deck.
A ship of the line mounting between 64 and 80 guns typically built with two gun decks.
skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Third Rate Ship Of The Line

skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A sailing ship with a sufficient numerically large gun armament to stand in the line of battle.
Moored barge providing a refuge for shipwrecked sailors or downed airmen, for example the air-sea rescue Cuckoo barges of WWII.
Shallow draft cargo vessel, often broad beamed and flat bottomed, powered by engine and or sail.
Oared fighting ship with ancillary sails.
A vessel for maintaining the buoys, hawsers and netting of a boom defence.

A type of clinker built wooden boat or ship held together with tendons of flexible wood such as roots and willow branches. Sewn boat construction techniques were used in many parts of the world prior to the development of metal fasteners.
A vessel, purpose-built or requisitioned, used for patrolling inland or coastal waters, e.g. Coastguard and Customs vessels.
Diesel engined submarine designed for long ocean voyages in search of enemy shipping.
A small warship engaged in patrolling coastal waters.
A vessel designed to operate below the surface of the sea.
Diesel engined submarine designed for long ocean voyages in search of enemy shipping.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.

The British designation for any German submarine built for military purposes. U-Boat is an anglicization of the German U-Boot, an abbreviation for Unterseeboot (Undersea Boat). In German U-Boot refers to any submarine.

An artificial harbour, constructed using prefabricated, concrete caissons, floating breakwaters and blockships, which were towed across the channel and assembled off the coast of Normandy to assist the allied forces after the D-Day landings in June 1944.
A pontoon element of a Mulberry Harbour that sits between the Spud Pontoons that allow oit to rise and fall with the tide.
A vessel used for transporting passengers or goods.

Any boat used to transfer crew from shore to an offlying vessel. May also carry the catch from a fishing boat to shore. Used as special boats by the Royal National Lifeboat Institution for conveying crew from the beach to a moored lifeboat.
A vessel designed principally for the carriage of fare paying passengers.
A vessel used for the transport of troops, either during wartime or for garrison duties in peacetime.
Small ocean going vessel fitted with loading doors in the bow. Used for ferrying tanks or other fighting vehicles to beach landings.
A flat bottomed boat or hollow float of which a number are used to support a temporary bridge over a river.
A barge with a lugsail rig commonly found on the River Thames in the late 19th century. Also a general term applied to the family of Deal luggers.
Flat-bottomed barge with shallow draft adapted for carrying bulk cargoes among the shoals of the Thames Estuary; usually spritsail-rigged, though other rigs were permissible.
A vessel designed or adapted to sweep and explode mined areas at sea without prior detection. (See Minehunter for vessels that detect and destroy individual mines).
A vessel designed or adapted for the task of keeping shipping lanes and ports free from the threat of naval mines. Use specific type where known. of
A barge with a lugsail rig commonly found on the River Thames in the late 19th century. Also a general term applied to the family of Deal luggers.
A vessel or craft used for catching or harvesting marine life by a variety of methods, use specific method where known.
Fishing smack specialised both for working shoal water and for trawling for shrimp, predominantly in and around Morecambe Bay.
Vessels of various types used specifically for providing an initial training in seamanship.
A training ship to which boys who had committed petty crimes were sentenced for 4 to 5 years to be reformed and trained as sailors.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War. The U Boat carried 12 mines but was otherwise unarmed with no deck gun or torpedo tubes.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War.

A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War. In addition to 18 mines, the U BOAT also carried 7 torpedoes in 3 tubes (2 bow/1 stern) and and 88mm or 105mm deck gun. A very successful design, UCIIs sank more than 1800 vessels.
A submarine built or adapted to carry mines and lay minefields.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War.
Flat bottomed, two-masted Dutch coastal vessel of the sixteenth to nineteenth centuries. Sometimes used as an English term for fluit, also a flat-bottomed vessel.
A ship used for laying cables (often telephone cables) along the sea bed.

High speed craft developed in the 20th Century which employ a wing-like structure mounted on struts below the hull which lift the vessel above the water line during forward motion, reducing drag and increasing speed.

A freelance boat used off the south and east coasts of England to provide passing ships with pilots, supplies, new anchors and other gear. Some helped sailing ships and barges get underway. Generally lug-rigged. Also used for fishing. Half decked.
A flat floating framework of planks or other materials used for supporting or carrying persons or goods.
Any vessel which has been hired or impressed for use by the Admiralty.
Any vessel involved in the catching or transportation of whales.
A craft employed to harpoon whales and bring them in to be processed.
Large warship equipped with turret mounted guns. Intended for independent action for trade protection, reconaissance and fire support for amphibious operations.
Large warship with no armour and a high speed. Used for reconnaisance and raids on enemy merchant shipping.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A type of small boat that was often carried, or towed by a larger vessel and used as a ship's boat. Dinghies can also be used recreationally as independent sailing vessels.
Craft intended for recreation and not intended for any commercial use.
An old ship converted for a variety of uses for which it is not required to move under its own power.
Demasted old ship used as a floating prison.
A vessel used for storing and issuing gunpowder, usually moored well away from the dockyard.
A demasted old ship used for storing items.
A demasted ship used exclusively for the storage of grain.
A small, single-masted merchant vessel, designed for maximum hold capacity and not for sailing ability.
A single decked cargo vessel carrying the majority of its cargo on deck.
A small fast warship powered by diesel or petrol engines. Built during World War II for coastal patrol work and anti shipping missions close in shore
Steam-powered torpedo boat developed in the early 20th century, and used in the First World War, a development of the torpedo boat destroyer, and distinct from the motor torpedo boat of WWII.
A small warship with a shallow draught. Used for patrol duties and shore bombardment on major rivers.
Late nineteenth and early twentieth century warship armed with guns and torpedoes.
A small sailing vessel armed with guns.
A sailing warship armed with one or more heavy howitzers or mortars and used for bombarding shore positions. The modern equivalent is the MONITOR.
A small warship built to operate close to the shore or in river estuaries. Used for shore bombardment and patrol duties.
Any vessel in use by the West India Company.

Small, light, fast, all-purpose craft designed primarily for inland use. Generally flat-bottomed, often with rocker; sharp bow, sharp or transom stern, lapstrake or carvel planking. Propelled by oars, sails or motor. Used for fishing and leisure.
A ship designed or modified to carry large numbers of mines and be able to lay them as a minefield.
A vessel used to carry supplies to warships.
A vessel with a type of rigging where the main driving sails are fixed to yards which lie square to the mast.
Two masted merchant vessel, rigged as a brig.
A passenger vessel equipped to carry a large number of emigrants usually with large third class decks and plenty of cargo space.
A vessel used to carry a pilot to a ship.
A fast, armed vessel, small in size, often used for despatches and mail.
Name given to a vessel of the East India Company. Usually large, well built and magnificently decorated.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War.
A small open boat, usually a very light craft propelled by a paddle.
Any small rigged warship below the sailing classification of frigate.
A vessel used to put down and pick up moorings on the sea bed for large vessels.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A ship designed specifically for the carriage of liquid cargoes, particularly oil.

skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Fifth Rate Ship Of The Line
skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A sailing frigate mounting 32 to 44 guns with the main battery on a single deck.
A fishing vessel specially designed to operate and trawl for the catching of bottom lying fish. Use with power where known.
Specially designed/fitted carriers of crew and/or supplies to offshore oil platforms.

skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: First Rate Ship Of The Line
skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A ship of the line mounting 100 guns or more on three gundecks.
A dredger that works by the use of suction.
A craft used to deepen shipping channels, harbours and other water courses.
British 'M' class submarine modified to carry and launch a small sea plane.

Privateer operating under government licence to act against enemy merchant shipping. The term is closely associated with North African privateers which operated off the Barbary coast prior to the mid-1800's.
A private fighting ship under a letter of marque or financed by subscribers, shareholders or individuals.
First practical submarine design. The first to use a combination of battery power and internal combustion engines.
A fishing vessel that catches fish by using a Seining net, that is a large net that is made to hang in the water by attaching weights along the bottom edge and counteracting floats along the top.
A mine countermeasure vessel built to detect and destroy individual naval mines. (See Minesweeper for vessels that sweep and explode mined areas at sea without prior detection).
A small vessel used to carry supplies and personnel to lighthouses.
Normally an unpowered vessel, moored in places where the construction of a lighthouse is impractical.
Any vessel operated by Trinity house masters and brethren in the maintenance of lighthouses, lightships, buoys and other navigation aids.
A craft used to deepen shipping channels, harbours and other water courses.

A fishing vessel which tows a dredger to gather oysters. This is an iron wedge shaped contrivance with a small net attached. Towed behind the vessel the iron wedge loosens the oysters and they fall into the net.

Small, light, fast, all-purpose craft designed primarily for inland use. Generally flat-bottomed, often with rocker; sharp bow, sharp or transom stern, lapstrake or carvel planking. Propelled by oars, sails or motor. Used for fishing and leisure.
A small warship mounting between 20 and 28 nine-pounder guns on a single deck.
skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Sixth Rate Ship Of The Line

skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A craft available for public hire for short journeys.
Transport vessel hired for the enforced removal of convicts to penal colonies abroad(e.g. Virginia, Australia), or for their return to Britain.
A small cargo craft, usually with two masts and a lateen rig.
A passenger vessel equipped with recreational facilities for long voyages for pleasure and sight seeing.
A flat bottomed vessel for receiving mud and other material from a dredger.
Two masted vessel with a square rig on both masts.
Transport vessel, usually a hired vessel of mercantile origin, used for the exchange of prisoners-of-war; sometimes, specifically a hospital ship with such prisoners.
A vessel used for carrying mines between warships and supply depots.
The characteristics of a sailing vessel where her type is determined by her masts and sails.
A barge with a lugsail rig commonly found on the River Thames in the late 19th century. Also a general term applied to the family of Deal luggers.
A heavily armoured capital ship with an armament composed entirely of large calibre guns and powered by stem turbines. In effect any battleship constructed after 1908.
A capital warship, often steam driven, heavily armoured with armament of large calibre guns deployed in turrets.

Small, light, fast, all-purpose craft designed primarily for inland use. Generally flat-bottomed, often with rocker; sharp bow, sharp or transom stern, lapstrake or carvel planking. Propelled by oars, sails or motor. Used for fishing and leisure.
A small warship powered by petrol or diesel engines and armed with torpedo tubes and small guns. Used for coastal anti shipping patrols and warfare in coastal waters.

The name given by the Allies during the Second World War to the German motor torpedo boat. E Boats carried two torpedo tubes and anti aircraft guns. They had a maximum speed of 39 knots and were considered superior to their Allied counterparts.
A vessel used for the transport of troops, either during wartime or for garrison duties in peacetime.

A fishing vessel which tows a dredger to gather oysters. This is an iron wedge shaped contrivance with a small net attached. Towed behind the vessel the iron wedge loosens the oysters and they fall into the net.
Two masted ship with fore and aft sails and two or more masts.
A vessel equipped with water tanks and pumps for fighting fires at sea or on the shore line.
A tug equipped with water tanks and pumps for fighting fires at sea or on the shore line.
French Banker, fishing vessel for the Grand Banks of Newfoundland, generally three-masted or of brig type, from the late 19th and 20th centuries.
Small vessel built or adapted to lay and recover anti submarine nets in harbours and anchorages.
A ship of the line mounting between 46 and 64 guns
skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Fourth Rate Ship Of The Line

skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: Cargo vessel specially constructed to carry bulk items such as grain. Does not include liquid cargoes.
Support or 'mother ship' used for the repair, maintenance and refuelling of submarines, specifically 20th century submarines.
A vessel used for carrying cargoes, fitted with defensive weapons. Use with specific type and form where known.
A vessel with equipment for processing dead marine life whilst still at sea.
A ship used to process whales into saleable products.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.

Dutch coaster of the late 17th to late 19th centuries with a square hull and 2 masts, generally having a heavy keel and round stern and bilges, although later examples were built on more refined lines.
Small cargo vessel used for short coastal voyages carrying a variety of cargoes.
A vessel (often small obsolete cruisers or pleasure steamers) equipped with batteries of anti aircraft guns as its main armament.
A sloop used for carrying weapons and ammunition to warships.
A vessel used for carrying weapons and ammunition to warships.

A fishing vessel which tows a dredger to gather oysters. This is an iron wedge shaped contrivance with a small net attached. Towed behind the vessel the iron wedge loosens the oysters and they fall into the net.
Old ship with a hull stripped to the water line and used as a platform for mounting the sheers used to lift masts into and out of sailing ships.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A vessel equipped with sealed tanks for transporting sewage and dumping it in deep water.
A cargo vessel designed to carry goods packed in large containers that can be loaded directly onto trains or lorries.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War. The U Boat had 5 torpedo tubes (4 bow/1 stern) and carried 10 torpedoes as well as an 88mm (or in some cases 105mm) deck gun.
A small galley or Dutch trading vessel similar to a barge in construction.
Flat bottomed vessel used for coasting on the River Humber.
A vessel for carrying a cargo of coal in bulk.
A small and powerful vessel used to assist vessels that are sinking or disabled at sea.
Three-masted fishing lugger built in and operating out of Yorkshire on the North Sea fisheries; so named as it was crewed by five joint owners, plus two or three paid hands.
A cargo vessel constructed in the U.S.A. during World War II from prefabricated sections by welding.
A barge fitted with a large tank in its hull for carried liquid cargoes.
A flush decked sailing warship with a single tier of guns.
A warship used to provide an escort to other warships or merchant ships against attack.
A small vessel used to attack other vessels and setting themselves and then the enemy alight.
Flat bottomed vessel used as a platform for a pile driver.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A ship belonging to a shipping company which carries passengers on a scheduled route usually applied to steam ships only.
A boat fashioned out of a section of tree trunk.
A boat used as private or domestic living accomodation.
A vessel with two masts, rigged as a ketch, used for coastal trading.

A short wide-beamed Dutch craft, often used as a fishing vessel or, in the 17th and 18th centuries, a privateer due to its manoeuvrability. Fitted with a ketch rig or variations thereof. Used extensively in the North Sea.
A barge with a lugsail rig commonly found on the River Thames in the late 19th century. Also a general term applied to the family of Deal luggers.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.

A freelance boat used off the south and east coasts of England to provide passing ships with pilots, supplies, new anchors and other gear. Some helped sailing ships and barges get underway. Generally lug-rigged. Also used for fishing. Half decked.
A ship acting as a base for smaller vessels and or submarines.
A ship in government use, used to carry naval or military stores.
A vessel used to carry away mud etc from a dredging machine and discharging it elsewhere.
A vessel carrying square sails on all masts and having three or more masts.
Single masted east coast trading vessel, ketch rigged or single masted.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A vessel with a single mast, fore and aft rigged.
A dredger which can act as its own hopper and can thus transport the mud which it picks up to dump it at the desired place.

A floating pier, comprised of a floating pierhead (SPUD) and a roadway resting on pontoons (BEETLES). The pierhead and roadway were capable of rising and falling with the tide enabling unloading of troops and equipment to occur at any state of the tide.
Concrete or steel pontoons that supported the Whale piers. They were moored in position using wires attsched to Kite anchors.
Vessel fitted with broadcasting equipment and used for off-shore radio programme transmission.

Transport vessel used for the repatriation of wounded troops, particularly during the First World War. Loosely described as "hospital ships", these ships were distinct from hospital ships proper by being defensively armed.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War. The U Boat carried 12 mines but was otherwise unarmed with no deck gun or torpedo tubes.
Sailing vessel generally with three masts square rigged on the foremast only.
Any vessel propelled by sail or power and used for pleasure and not plying for hire.
A large Mediterranean cargo or transport vessel of the 15th to 17th century, usually less than 400 tons.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War. The U Boat had 5 torpedo tubes (4 bow/1 stern) and carried 10 torpedoes as well as an 88mm (or in some cases 105mm) deck gun.
A craft on which is a machine for the loading and unloading of grain to and from a warehouse.

Generic name for a vessel used for work rather than for recreation, transportation, or military purposes. The vessels include tugs, barges, landing craft, dredgers and standby safety vessels. Use only were the exact type of vessel is unknown.
A barge with a spritsail rig commonly found on the River Thames.
A floatable concrete vessel, numbers of which made up the main breakwater of the Mulberry Harbours used to secure beachheads during the allied invasion of Europe in June 1944.

A floating breakwater in the form of a hollow cruciform tube 200 ft long by 25 ft high. Bombardons were designed to reduce the height of waves and formed the outer breakwater of the Mulberry harbours.
A small two masted craft, with oars as well as sails.

A freelance boat used off the south and east coasts of England to provide passing ships with pilots, supplies, new anchors and other gear. Some helped sailing ships and barges get underway. Generally lug-rigged. Also used for fishing. Half decked.
A vessel used for the underwater recovery of a ship and/or her contents or to assist vessels that are sinking.
A type of ferry that is designed to carry wheeled vehicles that can be driven on to and off the vessel.
A vessel designed for the transport of persons and goods from one place to another an a regular schedule of sailings. Use specific type were known.
A small vessel with a cabin as an integral part of its construction.

A type of motorboat that provides accommodation for crew and passengers within the crafts cabin. Cabin Cruisers are usually between 25 feet and 45 feet long in length and are not to be confused with cruise ships which are substantially larger.
A dredger which can act as its own hopper and can thus transport the mud which it picks up to dump it at the desired place.
A dredger that works by the use of suction.

A boat specifically designed for saving lives at sea, they can be either: a boat carried on a larger vessel for use in emergency evacuation of crew and passengers in the event of shipwreck or a boat built for the purpose of shore-to-sea rescue missions.
A specially equipped ship designed to care for survivors of stricken vessels and offshore drilling platforms. These ships were often converted naval trawlers or minesweepers.
Vehicle either adapted, or purpose-built, to operate on both land and sea, for example a tank or DUKW.
17th/18th century lightly armed privateer, usually either a 'Dunkirker' or Dutch.
A broad, flat-bottomed boat.
A self propelled vessel for deepening harbours using a continuous chain of buckets
A medium speed anti submarine escort vessel built from World War II
Small submarine with a two or three man crew. Used for special missions such as sinking warships in heavily defended harbours.
A large cargo vessel, either purpose-fitted or converted, for the transportation of slaves.
Large submarine intended to operate with a surface fleet at sea.
Any vessel propelled by sail or power and used for pleasure and not plying for hire.
The largest boat belonging to a sailing vessel, and usually the means of escape from a sinking ship; powered by oars.
Old ship converted to a floating coal store.
A small ferry powered by steam or diesel engine, which pull vessel from one side of a river crossing along submerged chains.
Generic term for fast sailing ships with schooner rig and fine hull lines.
Ketch or yawl rigged vessel with a large mizzen sail used in the west country.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A rescue tug equipped to care for survivors from torpedoed vessels and also available for towing to port ships damaged by enemy action.
A tug equipped with water tanks and pumps for fighting fires at sea or on the shore line.
A small stem powered warship with its hull protected by iron plates. Used to bombard targets onshore, especially in the Crimean War.
World War II naval support vessel usually converted from a mercantile or other warship type and used for the distillation of water by both the Royal Navy and the American Navy.
Any vessel designed for speed or endurance racing.

A small three-masted lateen-rigged vessel used exclusively in the Mediterranean from the 16th to the 19th century. Its hull was similar in form to that of a polacre but with an extended bow and stern.
A decked sailing vessel of shallow draft.
A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War.
A small vessel used to carry fresh water to ships at anchor or in harbour.
A single masted cutter built specifically for the prevention of smuggling and the enforcement of customs regulations.
A type of small boat that was often carried, or towed by a larger vessel and used as a ship's boat. Dinghies can also be used recreationally as independent sailing vessels.
A small vessel attached to a larger vessel (usually a warship) for general harbour duties such as taking crewmen ashore or fetching supplies.

skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Second Rate Ship Of The Line
skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A ship of the line mounting between 90 and 98 guns on three gun decks
A designed for the carriage of livestock usually cattle or sheep.
A hulk or obsolete vessel filled with cement or similar material and scuttled to block an entrance to a port or anchorage, usually as a defensive measure.

A prefabricated cargo vessel produced in large numbers by North American shipyards during World War II to replace shipping losses caused by German submarines and replacing the earlier 'LIBERTY' ships.
A vessel fitted with a catapult for launching an aircraft.

World War II-era British merchant ships used in convoys as an emergency stop-gap until sufficient escort carriers became available. They were equipped with a rocket-propelled catapult launching a single Hawker Sea Hurricane plane.
A fishing boat using nets which are free to move with the tides and currents.
A large Mediterranean cargo or transport vessel of the 15th to 17th century, usually less than 400 tons.
A small one masted craft, often using oars as well as sails.
Open decked vessel used for sight seeing or pleasure voyages.
Oared sailing vessel developed in the 15th century, the larger English version with secondary oars coming into use in the 16th century. Not to be confused with GALEAS.
A self propelled vessel for deepening harbours using a continuous chain of buckets

A small clinker-built craft, dating primarily from the 19th and early 20th centuries, fitted with oars and often a sail, usually found on the north-east coast of England and used for fishing or pilotage.
Small, heavily built coble, usually fitted with lugsail and oars, used on the north-east coast of England to assist, tow, pilot, provision or otherwise attend ships.
A three masted vessel with a square rig on the fore and main masts and lateen rig on the mizzen mast.
Three-masted fishing lugger built in and operating out of Yorkshire on the North Sea fisheries; so named as it was crewed by five joint owners, plus two or three paid hands.
A vessel with a lug sail rig. A lug is a four sided sail set on a lug or yard. Modified from square-rigged vessels from the late 17th century. Became a popular fishing boat rig in the 19th century.
A dredger with a grab hung on ropes or chains giving it unlimited depth of excavation. they can lift solids or pieces of wreckages and can work close to ships or to a dock wall.
A dredger with a grab hung on ropes or chains giving it unlimited depth of excavation. they can lift solids or pieces of wreckages and can work close to ships or to a dock wall.
A fishing vessel employed exclusively on the Grand Banks cod fisheries.
A single masted cutter built specifically for the prevention of smuggling and the enforcement of customs regulations.
Generic term for all vessels used to supply warships with any type of fuel.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A small warship (often adapted from a trawler or drifter) used to hunt enemy submarines.
Warship or trading vessel, three masted with a low forecastle.
A ship used for treating patients or moving them between a battle zone and a safe area.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A craft which is equipped for and performs hydrographic surveys.
Unpowered flat bottomed vessel, used for carrying cargo from ship to shore or as a working platform.
A small, fast boat used for carrying orders between sailing vessels.
Large cargo vessel specially built and equipped for handling large quantities of ore.
A vessel containing equipment for reducing the magnetic structure of a ship's hull making it immune to magnetic mines.
Auxiliary or support vessel used for the repair of naval craft.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
A capital warship lightly armoured but with the same gun armament and stem turbine propulsion as a DREADNOUGHT BATTLESHIP.
Landing craft designed for carrying military vehicles and used for beach landings and as a ferry.
Shallow draft vessel used to carry ammunition.
A ship used for treating patients or moving them between a battle zone and a safe area.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.

Any one of the cargo vessels of that name belonging to the different Baltic nations, in use from the mid 18th to the early 20th century. Usually ketch-rigged. Not to be confused with the oared GALEASS.
A vessel for policing fishing grounds and fishing vessels.
French warship broadly equivalent to the English 4th to 6th Rates, with as few as 4 to as many as 50 guns.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.

A freelance boat used off the south and east coasts of England to provide passing ships with pilots, supplies, new anchors and other gear. Some helped sailing ships and barges get underway. Generally lug-rigged. Also used for fishing. Half decked.
A craft used to retrieve torpedoes after practice firings.
Cargo vessel or warship captured by a warship or privateer of a hostile nation in time of war, usually taken into that nation�s mercantile, privateering or naval service.
Small two or three masted ship used as trading vessels in the medieval mediterranean.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
Generic name for a small steam or power boat often carried on board a larger vessel.
A steam vessel propelled by rotating propellers called screws.
Large inshore craft with a retractable loading ramp in the bow. Used to carry tanks ashore for a beach landing or as a tank ferry.
A German coastal attack submarine in use during the First World War.
Small square rigged ship with a narrow overhanging stern.
A small coastal fishing vessel peculiar to Kent, cutter rigged.
Support or 'mother ship' used for the repair, maintenance and refuelling of submarines, specifically 20th century submarines.
Small coastal vessel.
A small anti submarine escort vessel built in World War II
A vessel used for inspecting vessels entering port during wartime.
An obsolete vessel used as a weapons target.

Any boat used to transfer crew from shore to an offlying vessel. May also carry the catch from a fishing boat to shore. Used as special boats by the Royal National Lifeboat Institution for conveying crew from the beach to a moored lifeboat.
A vessel used as a platform for flying a barrage balloon from.
A sailing vessel, generally with three masts, square rigged on the fore and main mast.
French coasting vessel
Flat bottomed vessel used for loading colliers in the north east, similar to a lighter.
A vessel used to transport bombs and depth charges from the shore to flying boats during the Second World War.
A buoy with a compartment into which shipwrecked mariners could climb to await rescue.
A vessel for carrying messages between warships.
Dutch galliot-rigged vessel used mostly for river trade in Holland but sometimes also for coasting or short sea-going voyages
A craft built with the intention of demonstrating or advertising a novel design feature.

A six oared rowing boat which originated in Cornwall in the 18th century. Originally used as a pilot vessel, useage adapted to include cargo vessel and lifeboat. Today gigs are used in sporting events.

A Dutch barge-like sailing vessel whose shallow draught enables it to navigate in shallow water not accessible by other vessels. Used for fishing or carriage of cargo in the 19th century this type of vessel was adapted for use as a type of leisure craft.
A major warship equipped with a clear flat deck to provide a landing and taking off place for aircraft.
A two masted craft which is square rigged on the foremast and fore and aft rigged on the main mast.
A small craft of wickerwork covered with waterproof material.
A steam vessel propelled by a pair of paddle wheels amidships or a single wheel astern, driven by a reciprocating engine.
A barge with a crane or sheer legs used for moving and maintaining mooring buoys.

A German coastal minelaying submarine in use during the First World War. In addition to 18 mines, the U BOAT also carried 7 torpedoes in 3 tubes (2 bow/1 stern) and and 88mm or 105mm deck gun. A very successful design, UCIIs sank more than 1800 vessels.

Originally denoting a Byzantine vessel, powered by oars, from the 6th century onwards, this developed into a large Mediterranean ship with oars and a single mast, used for trade or for war transport, (e.g. during the Crusades) until the 15th century.
An air-cushioned vehicle capable of travelling over land or water.
A small light vessel with a schooner rig often used for fishing.
A multihulled craft consisting of two hulls, or vakas, which are usually joined together by a frame or other structure, formed of akas. Catamarans can be sail or engine powered.
Mercantile vessel converted during the First World War for the purposes of examining and boarding enemy vessels.
A small cargo vessel used on the West coast of Scotland.
North European medieval merchant vessel.

A term for any vessel powered principally by sail and oars, or oars alone, used especially for war. As a broad term it may refer to Roman galleys, or more specifically to an Anglo-Saxon or Norse warship of the Viking Age.
A three masted fully rigged warship armed with 24 to 38 guns on a single gundeck.
Small fishing craft used mainly for fishing, cutter or ketch rigged.
A small cargo boat found on the River Severn in England, with a mast which could be taken down to allow the boat to pass under low bridges.
A shallow draft vessel used to carry a crane.
A small and powerful vessel used to assist vessels that are sinking or disabled at sea.
